skippedSources.name: Checkonchain
skippedSources.reason: Q&A submission form only, zero sentences of content

items.title: US strikes Iran; peace talks resume Tuesday; 500 ships stuck at Hormuz
items.idea: Over the weekend the US struck Iranian missile/drone storage + coastal radar sites in retaliation for Iran's Hormuz shipping attacks. Kuwait and Bahrain reported incoming missiles/drones. Oil jumped: Brent ~$73, WTI ~$70.75 — then Trump signaled US-Iran talks resume June 30 (today), stabilizing risk appetite. ~500 commercial vessels are still waiting to transit Hormuz. Iran's parliament MoU ratification is pending in the June 28-30 window. The ceasefire framed as 'Epic Fury' began Feb 28; WTI is now back below pre-war levels — the clearest peace-dividend signal yet.
items.angle: This is the single biggest macro input today. Peace talks succeeding = oil down + risk on. A breakdown = oil spike + tech selloff. The $3.93/gal gas price is holding below the wartime $4.56 peak. NFP Thursday compounds this.
items.tweetA:
500 ships waiting at Hormuz. US struck Iran over the weekend. Talks resume TODAY. WTI already back below pre-war levels — the market is pricing a deal.

If it holds: oil down, spreads tighten, Nasdaq continues. If it breaks: we go back to $90 oil in 48 hours.

This is the only trade today.
items.tweetB:
The peace dividend is hiding in plain sight. WTI closed BELOW where it was before the Iran war started Feb 28.

Brent +1.6% overnight on strikes, then faded. The market has already priced the ceasefire — not the risk of it unraveling.

That asymmetry worries me more than the headline.

items.title: May PCE came in hot: 4.1% headline, 3.4% core — market pricing possible 2026 hike
items.idea: Thursday's May PCE print was hot: 4.1% headline, 3.4% core. Markets are now pricing the possibility of a 2026 rate hike. Warsh speaks at a Europe forum Wednesday (Sintra) — first read on his post-FOMC tone. Thursday NFP is the decisive labor read of the week. Below 4.5% on the 10Y = money broadens beyond tech; at/above 4.5% = gravitates back to tech (per Schwab). June scorecard: Nasdaq -~6%, S&P -2%, Dow +1% = the defensive/value rotation defined the entire month.
items.angle: Hot PCE + possible hike pricing is the hidden regime change. The June scoreboard already shows Dow beating Nasdaq by 700bps for the month. If Warsh confirms hawkish at Sintra, that rotation accelerates.
items.tweetA:
May PCE: 4.1% headline, 3.4% core. Market pricing a 2026 hike.

June scorecard: Nasdaq -6%. Dow +1%. That's 700bps in one month — the rotation the Fed was trying to engineer.

Warsh speaks at Sintra Wednesday. NFP Thursday. If both are hawkish, the tech/value spread keeps widening.
items.tweetB:
Everyone is focused on whether the Iran deal holds. But May PCE just printed 4.1% headline, 3.4% core.

This is the real overhang. Warsh at Sintra + NFP Thursday. Two data points in 48h that will tell you whether this summer rally has fuel — or whether we're reliving Q1 2022.

items.title: M2 money supply growing at fastest pace in 5 years
items.idea: US money supply is expanding at its fastest pace in 5 years. This is the macro undercurrent beneath the surface debate about PCE and Fed hikes — liquidity is actually expanding even as the policy rate stays elevated. Prior M2 surges (2020-2021) led to asset price inflation with a 12-18 month lag.
items.angle: M2 expanding at 5-year highs while Fed talks hawkish is the classic setup for a stealth liquidity wave. Bitcoin, gold, and real assets historically front-run M2 acceleration.
items.tweetA:
M2 growing at the fastest pace in 5 years. And the market is debating whether the Fed hikes.

Both can be true: rates elevated AND liquidity expanding. That's the 2025-26 regime in one sentence.

Last time M2 grew this fast, BTC was at $15K. Connect the dots.
items.tweetB:
The Fed talks hawkish. PCE is hot. And M2 is growing at the fastest clip since 2021.

The plumbing is loose. The surface is tight. That combination historically ends with inflation in asset prices, not disinflation in consumer prices.

Charts inside — worth opening. $GLD $BTC

charts.desc: M2 growth chart vs prior cycles — paywalled

items.title: Gold at $3,955 hunting for a floor — market has rediscovered yield
items.idea: Gold near $3,955 has lost its $4,000 handle and USD/JPY is above 162.25 — the author frames these as 'two expressions of the same storm.' The dollar is rewarded because it pays; the Fed only needed to convince the market that hikes are back on the table (not actually hike) to hurt gold. Correlation between gold and USD/JPY: 0.60. $3,900 is next ledge; $3,900-$4,100 is 'not a buy zone, just the market listening for the next crack.' Warsh at Sintra Wednesday, NFP, and June CPI are the three catalysts that determine whether $3,900 holds.
items.angle: Gold without a floor is the tell on the macro regime. The dollar paying real yield kills the gold thesis until something breaks — watch June CPI. A softer print = gold's first opportunity to build a base.
items.tweetA:
Gold broke $4,000. USD/JPY above 162. Both are the same trade: the dollar is earning yield again.

The Fed didn't need to hike. It only needed to take cuts off the table. That changed the arithmetic for gold.

Next line is $3,900. June CPI decides whether it holds. $GLD
items.tweetB:
Gold at $3,955 is 'not building a base — it's walking across rotten floorboards.'

Gold/USD-JPY correlation is 0.60. When both are moving together, the driver is the same: rates and dollar in command.

Warsh Sintra + NFP + June CPI in the next 10 days. Gold is a hostage to every print.

items.title: Equity funding costs at exceptional mid-year levels — the frenzy financing the calm
items.idea: The equity market looks calm on the surface but equity funding costs have surged to exceptional mid-year levels, suggesting demand for leveraged long exposure is beginning to test dealer balance sheet capacity. GS's Robert Quinn flagged SPX total-return futures as the key warning signal: investors are paying materially more to maintain long exposure even as equities remain elevated. The SPX vs Russell 2000 funding spread reveals the leverage is concentrated in large-cap tech and semis — not spread across the broader market. Risk: when a marginal counterparty can't fund the position, leveraged ETFs, options hedges, swaps and dealer balance sheets can all start moving in the same direction simultaneously.
items.angle: This is the structural fragility hidden beneath the S&P 7,440 surface. The concentration of leverage in a handful of large-cap names means the exit is narrow. GS is already flagging this via futures pricing.
items.tweetA:
S&P at 7,440. VIX contained. Everyone relax.

Except: equity funding costs just hit exceptional mid-year levels. GS's Robert Quinn is flagging SPX total-return futures — investors are paying materially more to stay long.

The leverage is concentrated in 5-10 names. That exit is very narrow.
items.tweetB:
The calm is real. The funding is not.

The SPX vs Russell 2000 funding spread says the leverage isn't in the market — it's in a handful of large-cap tech and semi names.

When the marginal counterparty can't fund the position, leveraged ETFs + swaps + dealer books all move the same direction. That's the flash crash setup.

items.title: Month-end AND quarter-end FX rebalancing TODAY — GBP/USD is the vehicle
items.idea: June 30 is simultaneously month-end and quarter-end — a double rebalancing event. Foreign investors who own US equities with FX hedges must buy dollars back when the equity book falls (the hedge becomes too large relative to the reduced asset base). After Nasdaq's -6% June, foreign equity holders have a material dollar-buying need. GBP/USD is often the preferred expression: enough volume to absorb large orders, but not the bottomless liquidity of EUR/USD, so it moves more cleanly. The London 4pm WMR fix is the moment.
items.angle: Mechanical flow at a predictable time with a known direction is an edge. After Nasdaq -6% in June, the size of the dollar rebalancing bid is real. Watch GBP/USD around the 4pm London fix.
items.tweetA:
Today is month-end AND quarter-end. After Nasdaq -6% in June, foreign equity holders need to buy dollars at the WMR 4pm London fix.

GBP/USD is the preferred expression — liquid enough to absorb, but not bottomless like EUR/USD.

This isn't a thesis. It's mechanics. Watch the fix. $GBPUSD
items.tweetB:
The month-end fix is the market's old clockwork. Everyone can see it coming — the passengers still have to get off at the same station.

This quarter-end is bigger: Nasdaq was down 6%. That's a lot of hedge to unwind. Dollar buying is NOT a macro bet. It's the hedge being pulled back to match a smaller equity base.

items.title: USD/JPY at 161.90 — strongest vs yen since Christmas Eve 1986; entering intervention-risk territory
items.idea: USD/JPY broke 161.90, the highest level against the yen since December 24, 1986. Critically, Japanese yields have been rising yet the yen still can't catch a bid — implying the market is trading dollar strength and carry demand, not Japan's marginal yield improvement. Citi opened a short at 159.10 in May and closed it at a loss on Monday. Dark Side of the Boom: 'the path of least resistance is to hold yen exposure rather than chase higher — it could go 150 points higher but eventually 400-500 lower.' AUD/NZD short working from 1.2230 toward 1.2165.
items.angle: Crowded elevator metaphor is right. At 162, the asymmetry shifts from momentum to risk/reward for yen longs. Watch for any fast, one-sided move — that's the intervention trigger, not a specific level.
items.tweetA:
USD/JPY at 161.90. Last time the yen was this weak: Christmas Eve 1986.

JGB yields are rising. The BoJ is hiking. Yet the yen is still falling.

That tells you this has nothing to do with Japan's fundamentals. It's dollar carry. And the elevator is crowded. MoF has every reason to hit the trapdoor.
items.tweetB:
Citi opened USD/JPY short at 159.10. Closed it Monday at a loss with a perfectly rational explanation: 'the MoF can stay patient longer than we can remain short.'

Strategist trades are often Monopoly money with Bloomberg terminals. The actual asymmetry is now in the other direction. 162 is the danger zone.

items.title: Japan's real effective exchange rate collapse — rising yields + falling currency = EM-style loss of confidence
items.idea: Bloomberg's Authers highlights that Japan's situation is anomalous: JGB yields have been rising relative to Treasuries (which should strengthen yen) but the yen is still falling. The combination of rising bond yields AND a falling currency, 'often seen in EM crises,' implies loss of confidence in Japan's debt sustainability. Japan's real effective exchange rate has weakened 'spectacularly' over 40 years. Kit Juckes of SocGen calls the yen 'untradeable.' Prime Minister Takaichi wants a weak yen to help exporters — but even nationalistic governments have limits.
items.angle: The EM crisis framing is the most alarming part. Japan is not EM. But if its bond market is behaving like one — yields up, currency down — something structural may be breaking. This is the late-stage carry trade and JGB crisis scenario in slow motion.
items.tweetA:
Japan: JGB yields rising. Yen falling. That combination — rising rates AND depreciating currency — is what EM crises look like.

For 40 years Japan was the EM anti-pattern. Safe haven, deflation, strong yen vs fundamentals.

If that framing is breaking, the global carry trade unwind is not a tail risk. It's a when.
items.tweetB:
USD/JPY 161.90. Japan's real effective exchange rate at multi-decade lows. Prime Minister Takaichi WANTS a weak yen for exporters.

SocGen calls the yen 'untradeable.' Citi surrendered its short.

At some point the weak-yen-as-policy becomes a domestic crisis — pension purchasing power, import inflation, social unrest. Japan is threading a needle.

charts.desc: Japan REER chart showing decades of purchasing power erosion — charts inside worth opening

items.title: ECB at Sintra: core inflation stuck above 2% through 2028 — September is a judgment call
items.idea: The ECB heads into Sintra with two contradictory inflation signals: oil prices have retreated sharply (giving doves a clean script), but core inflation remains structurally elevated. Even under the ECB staff's milder scenario, core peaks near 2.6% and stays above the 2% target through 2028 — two years of above-target core. The September meeting is shaping up as a judgment call between softer oil (surface disinflation) and stickier core (wages, services, domestic demand). A Taylor rule framework suggests the policy rate should be moving higher, not lower.
items.angle: ECB dovish pivot is the consensus. ECB hawkish surprise at Sintra via Lagarde would reprice EUR rates sharply. The core-above-2%-through-2028 finding from ECB's own staff is the key number.
items.tweetA:
The ECB is heading into Sintra with oil down (dovish cover) and core inflation stuck above 2% through 2028 (Taylor rule screaming).

The market is pricing cuts. The ECB's own staff projections say core doesn't return to target until 2029 at the earliest.

Someone is wrong. Find out Wednesday. $EUR $EURUSD
items.tweetB:
Oil fell. Headline inflation looks better. The ECB doves are ready.

Except: wages are sticky, services are still hot, and core inflation under the ECB's mild scenario peaks at 2.6% and stays above 2% through 2028.

Lagarde at Sintra could be the ECB's 'higher for longer' moment. September is not a done deal.

items.title: Supreme Court 5-4: Can't fire Fed governors — but CAN fire SEC and CFTC officials at will
items.idea: The Supreme Court ruled 5-4 that Trump cannot fire Fed governor Lisa Cook without due process. Markets barely moved — largely expected. But the more important ruling is the other side: Trump CAN fire CFTC and SEC officials essentially at will. Kevin Warsh has already replaced Powell as chairman. The real action is now at the SEC and CFTC: both have been crypto-friendly (BTC ETFs via SEC, perpetual crypto futures via CFTC, prediction markets via CFTC), and a politically-controlled SEC/CFTC could accelerate or reverse that direction. A future party change could mean 'very swift and drastic regulatory shifts.'
items.angle: The headline is 'Fed independence preserved.' The real story is 'SEC and CFTC are now fully politically controllable.' That's the biggest structural change for crypto regulation in years — and it cuts both ways.
items.tweetA:
5-4: Trump can't fire Fed governors. Markets shrugged.

But the same ruling says he CAN fire SEC and CFTC commissioners at will.

The Fed is special. Everything else isn't. A presidency-controlled SEC is the most important crypto regulatory development this year — and almost nobody is talking about it. $BTC $ETH
items.tweetB:
The headlines say 'Fed independence preserved.' True.

But buried in the same rulings: the president can now fire SEC and CFTC officials at will. Both agencies have been driving the crypto bull case (BTC ETFs, perp futures clearance, prediction markets).

Next administration can flip this. The regulatory moat is now political.

items.title: Dow above 52,000 for the first time — June defined by defensive/value rotation
items.idea: The Dow closed at 52,182.74, its first-ever close above 52,000. Alphabet joined the Dow (replacing Verizon) and surged ~5% on debut. June's scorecard: Nasdaq -~6%, S&P -2%, Dow +1% — a nearly 700bps divergence in one month. Below 4.5% on the 10Y = money broadens into value/cyclicals; at/above 4.5% = reverts to tech (Schwab). The AI trade bounced hard on the Iran de-escalation: Astera Labs +16%, KLA +12%, Applied Materials +11%, SMH +3%+, Tesla +8.5%.
items.angle: Dow record + Nasdaq -6% for the month is the value rotation thesis validating in real time. Alphabet entering the Dow is symbolic: even as tech leads AI, the 'defensive bluechip' framing is capturing flows.
items.tweetA:
Dow above 52,000 for the first time in history.

June scorecard: Dow +1%. Nasdaq -6%. S&P -2%.

The market is telling you something. Either the AI trade is broken, or value is finally getting its rotation. Either way, Schwab's call — below 4.5% on 10Y = broadening — is playing out exactly as described.
items.tweetB:
Alphabet entered the Dow today (replacing Verizon) and jumped 5%. The Dow hit 52,000 for the first time.

The irony: the stock that was 'too expensive for the Dow' just became the symbol of a new Dow record in a month when Nasdaq was down 6%.

The composition of 'the market' is quietly shifting.

items.title: TSMC-Winbond reported WoW stacking collaboration to fix AI's memory bottleneck
items.idea: Industry reports suggest TSMC is working with Winbond to develop a localized DRAM supply chain in Taiwan using wafer-on-wafer (WoW) stacking technology. WoW uses hybrid bonding to stack memory wafers directly with logic wafers — creating extremely dense copper interconnects that shorten data travel distance, improve bandwidth, reduce latency, and lower power consumption. TSMC traditionally relies on Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron. Winbond doesn't need to displace them — it only needs to become 'a useful additional lane in an increasingly congested memory system.' The thesis: AI chips are becoming faster than the memory systems feeding them.
items.angle: This is the $WINBOND / $TSM angle on the AI memory bottleneck thesis. If the AI boom is limited by memory bandwidth rather than FLOPS, Micron (HBM) and now Winbond become more interesting than more accelerators. $MU $2308.TW
items.tweetA:
Everyone is building faster AI chips. Almost nobody is solving the bottleneck feeding them.

TSMC reportedly working with Winbond on wafer-on-wafer DRAM stacking. Memory directly on logic. Shorter distance, more bandwidth, less power.

The next AI constraint isn't compute design. It's the road between the engine and the fuel tank. $MU $NVDA
items.tweetB:
AI capex has been obsessed with leading-edge logic. But what if the binding constraint is memory?

TSMC + Winbond wafer-on-wafer stacking = DRAM physically bonded to logic wafers. The traffic jam between processor and memory gets cleared.

Winbond doesn't need to beat Samsung. It just needs to become a second lane on a congested highway. $2308.TW

items.title: Anthropic renegotiates Amazon: compute-hours → token pricing; Amazon evaluating OpenAI and Nova as alternatives
items.idea: Anthropic renegotiated its pricing arrangement with Amazon AWS, switching from compute-hours to per-token billing — significantly more expensive for Amazon. In response, Amazon is evaluating whether to substitute OpenAI's models and its own Nova models in its products (Alexa for Shopping, Kiro, Quick). Historical deal terms: Anthropic forks over 50% of its gross profits to Amazon when AWS sells its models, AND pays for cloud hosting. Anthropic expected to pay cloud providers $1.9 billion in 2026. Despite this, Anthropic is asserting pricing power ahead of its anticipated IPO.
items.angle: This is the AI platform leverage war playing out between model companies and their cloud distributors — same dynamic as OpenAI/Microsoft. Anthropic is using its IPO timing to extract better terms. $AMZN
items.tweetA:
Anthropic switched Amazon from compute-hours to per-token pricing. Amazon costs go up materially.

Amazon's response: evaluating OpenAI and its own Nova models as substitutes.

Anthropic was paying 50% of gross profits to Amazon + $1.9B in cloud costs in 2026. Now it's extracting better terms ahead of its IPO. The power dynamic just flipped. $AMZN
items.tweetB:
The Anthropic-Amazon pricing fight is the same movie as OpenAI-Microsoft, one year later.

Model companies start as grateful distribution partners. Then they hit scale. Then they renegotiate.

Anthropic goes from 'AWS is our primary cloud' to 'pay us per token.' Amazon goes from 'our premier AI partner' to 'let's try Nova.' That's platform leverage 101.

items.title: Amazon invested $50B in OpenAI AND $25B more in Anthropic — dual-hedging the frontier model war
items.idea: Amazon has now committed up to $50 billion in OpenAI (with OpenAI using AWS infrastructure and Amazon getting access to OpenAI's tech) AND invested an additional $25 billion in Anthropic on top of its original $4 billion. This means Amazon is simultaneously the primary infrastructure partner for both leading frontier model companies. AWS executives worry 'too many Amazon products are powered by Anthropic rather than Amazon's own Nova models.' Amazon engineers have been distilling Anthropic models proactively, fearing future price increases.
items.angle: Amazon is running the play that hyperscalers always run: fund everyone, own the infrastructure, build the alternative. The risk is that both Anthropic and OpenAI squeeze you on pricing as they scale. $AMZN is the infrastructure toll road — now being taxed by its own tenants.
items.tweetA:
Amazon: $50B in OpenAI. $25B more in Anthropic. Plus its own Nova models.

This isn't a bet — it's a hedge. Amazon doesn't care who wins the model war. It wants to own the pipes that carry the winner's traffic.

The risk: both Anthropic and OpenAI are now repricing upward. The toll road is getting taxed by its own tenants. $AMZN
items.tweetB:
AWS engineers are proactively distilling Anthropic models 'in case they get more expensive.'

That tells you everything. The original partnership (Amazon infrastructure for Anthropic scale) is becoming adversarial.

$50B OpenAI deal + $25B Anthropic + Nova in-house = Amazon is hedging three different AI futures simultaneously. Expensive insurance.

items.title: Andy Jassy alerted White House to Anthropic model jailbreak → US government halted global distribution of Mythos and Fable on June 12
items.idea: Amazon CEO Andy Jassy raised concerns with White House officials about security risks in Anthropic's Mythos and Fable models. That call contributed to the White House prohibiting foreign nationals from accessing those models — which then led Anthropic to suspend use of the models more broadly ('The Blip 2.0'). This happened June 12. Context: the event coincided with the deteriorating Amazon-Anthropic pricing negotiation.
items.angle: One CEO's call to the White House caused a global AI model distribution halt. That's unprecedented leverage over AI companies. And it happened in the middle of a pricing dispute. Worth watching as a regulatory risk pattern.
items.tweetA:
Amazon CEO called the White House about Anthropic model security risks. White House banned foreign nationals from Anthropic's Mythos + Fable models. Anthropic suspended them globally.

This happened June 12. During an ongoing pricing dispute between the two companies.

Model distribution is now a government switch — not a commercial decision. That's new.
items.tweetB:
Buried in the Anthropic-Amazon story: Andy Jassy flagged an Anthropic model jailbreak to the White House in June.

Result: US government halted global distribution of two frontier models for weeks.

If competitors can trigger government intervention in each other's distribution, the AI landscape just got geopolitical in a way nobody priced.

items.title: OpenAI $122B raise + Anthropic $30B raise; both filed for IPOs; global VC tracking $4T record
items.idea: OpenAI raised $122 billion — a single round larger than every quarterly VC deal previously recorded combined, and more than 4x the next-largest deal. Anthropic raised $30 billion. Both have filed for IPOs that 'will likely determine whether lofty AI valuations can continue.' AI accounts for ~30% of VC-backed unicorns. Global M&A deal value projected to exceed $4 trillion in 2026 (most since 2021), with $2.6T already announced in H1, majority from $5B+ deals. GS's Ben Snider: 100 IPOs/year now vs. 400 in 1999 — elevated dollar volume but 'far from euphoric sentiment.'
items.angle: The IPO pipeline for OpenAI + Anthropic is THE valuation test for the AI cycle. If they trade well post-IPO, it sustains the VC funding flywheel for every AI startup. If they disappoint, the 30% of unicorns that are AI start repricing.
items.tweetA:
OpenAI's $122B raise is larger than every quarterly VC deal in history combined. Anthropic did $30B.

Both have filed for IPOs. GS notes 100 IPOs/year now vs. 400 in 1999 — not dot-com mania yet.

But the receptions for these two mega-IPOs will decide whether the entire AI VC flywheel keeps spinning. $AI accounts for 30% of all VC unicorns.
items.tweetB:
Global VC on track for a $4T record in 2026. But it's not broad-based euphoria — it's one sector, one cycle.

OpenAI: $122B. Anthropic: $30B. Two companies = more capital than any quarterly VC market ever recorded.

If either IPO underperforms, the exit strategy for every other AI unicorn gets repriced. That's the systemic risk hiding in the bull case.

charts.desc: VC deal value by quarter — OpenAI $122B vs all-time quarterly records; IPOX-100 vs S&P 500 chart — worth opening

items.title: Strategy (MSTR) unveils Digital Credit Capital Framework: $2B buyback + BTC liquidity program to fix sub-1 mNAV
items.idea: MicroStrategy/Strategy (MSTR) unveiled a 'Digital Credit Capital Framework': authorizes up to $2B in buybacks AND a program allowing future BTC sales to support liquidity — a direct response to MSTR trading at sub-1 mNAV (approximately 0.72). MSTR surged +12.6% on the announcement. This directly addresses the broken capital-raise flywheel (MSTR's business model relied on issuing equity/debt above mNAV to buy BTC; below mNAV the model breaks).
items.angle: The mNAV discount was the existential risk to the MSTR model — now they're buying back equity to close the gap instead of diluting. If BTC rallies and mNAV recovers above 1, the reflexive loop restarts. $MSTR $BTC
items.tweetA:
Strategy just authorized $2B in buybacks and a BTC liquidity program. The market is calling this a fix for the broken mNAV flywheel.

MSTR was at 0.72 mNAV. The capital-raise machine needs mNAV above 1 to work. Below 1, you're destroying value every time you issue equity.

Buybacks = the new weapon. MSTR +12.6%. $BTC
items.tweetB:
MicroStrategy's original playbook: issue equity above mNAV → buy BTC → BTC rises → mNAV rises → issue more equity. A perpetual flywheel.

That flywheel broke when mNAV hit 0.72. Today's 'Digital Credit Capital Framework' (buybacks + BTC sales for liquidity) is the emergency patch.

Whether it works depends entirely on what BTC does next. The model is still circular.

items.title: BTC at ~$60K; $300M shorts liquidated; Bitmine holds 5.7M ETH (4.7% of supply); SOL leading at +4.7%
items.idea: BTC recovered to ~$59,872-$60,137 (+0.89-1.00%), holding the $58,189 floor. Over $300M was liquidated in 24h with SHORTS taking the majority — a short squeeze driving the bounce. ETH at ~$1,594-$1,610 (+2.52%). SOL at ~$74.24-$74.93 (+4.42-4.69%), leading the majors. Fear & Greed still at 'Extreme Fear.' Van de Poppe: BTC breakout above $61,000 'very likely.' Key levels: $58,189 = floor, $61,000 = target, $62,457 = 200-week MA. Bitmine (BMNR) holds 5,700,040 ETH = 4.7% of total supply.
items.angle: Short squeeze mechanics vs. genuine recovery. Fear & Greed at Extreme Fear during a price bounce is a divergence. Bitmine holding 4.7% of ETH supply is the most underreported accumulation story in crypto right now.
items.tweetA:
BTC bounced to $60K on short liquidations. $300M shorts blown up in 24h.

Fear & Greed: still 'Extreme Fear.' Retail and whale Binance traders LOWERED long positions.

This isn't a new bid — it's a short squeeze. The bounce is real but the conviction isn't there yet. Watch $61K (Van de Poppe) + $62,457 (200-week MA). $BTC
items.tweetB:
Bitmine (BMNR) holds 5,700,040 ETH. That's 4.7% of the entire circulating supply.

For context: Grayscale at its peak held ~3% of BTC.

One company quietly accumulating 4.7% of ETH while Fear & Greed is at 'Extreme Fear' and ETH trades at $1,600. The structural bid is in place. The narrative just hasn't caught up. $ETH $BMNR

items.title: Russia's 'unfriendly crypto' bill targeting USDC and BNB took effect June 29 + mandatory exchange licensing
items.idea: Russia's legislation targeting 'unfriendly' cryptocurrency (specifically USDC and BNB) took effect on June 29, 2026. The law also introduces mandatory exchange licensing. This is a direct counter-sanction mechanism: Russia restricting dollar-denominated stablecoins. USDC is issued by Circle under US regulatory framework. The targeting of stablecoins rather than BTC is significant — it implies Russia views BTC as neutral but USDC/BNB as dollar-proxies to be blocked.
items.angle: Russia de-dollarizing crypto is the mirror of the CLARITY Act in the US. Two regulatory regimes pulling in opposite directions. USDC's utility as a truly global dollar-proxy is being challenged by nation-states.
items.tweetA:
Russia's 'unfriendly crypto' bill took effect yesterday. Targets: USDC, BNB. Not BTC.

The logic: BTC is stateless. USDC is a digital dollar. BNB is Binance's dollar-linked ecosystem.

Russia is treating crypto exactly like traditional sanctions evasion — block the dollar-proxies, use the stateless asset. That's a surprisingly sophisticated regulatory distinction. $USDC $BNB $BTC
items.tweetB:
US is debating the CLARITY Act. Russia just made USDC and BNB illegal.

Two regulatory regimes, completely opposite directions, converging on the same insight: crypto is geopolitical now.

BTC escapes both because it's truly neutral. Everything else picks a side by design. $USDC $BNB

items.title: CLARITY Act Section 604 under fire — ~100 Catholic bishops + anti-trafficking groups; White House July 4 target at risk
items.idea: CLARITY Act Section 604 is facing opposition from approximately 100 Catholic bishops plus an anti-trafficking advocacy group. The math for passage requires 7 Democratic votes; the White House is targeting a July 4 deadline. The bishops' opposition is unexpected because crypto legislation traditionally falls along economic/technology lines, not moral/religious ones. The anti-trafficking argument is that the provision could facilitate illicit payments. This coalition could flip the vote-math needed for passage.
items.angle: Political risk for US crypto legislation is rising. The 7-Democrat math was always tight; a church + NGO coalition is exactly the kind of outside pressure that flips moderate Dems. July 4 deadline looking shaky.
items.tweetA:
~100 Catholic bishops are opposing Section 604 of the CLARITY Act. An anti-trafficking group joined them.

This is the unexpected coalition that breaks crypto legislation. Not Republicans. Not finance traditionalists. The church.

The bill needed 7 Dem votes. This kind of moral-framing campaign is exactly what flips them. July 4 target is at real risk.
items.tweetB:
The CLARITY Act's problem isn't the crypto industry. It's the framing.

Once your bill gets labeled 'enables trafficking' by bishops and NGOs, the 7-Democrat senators you needed become unavailable for photo-ops at the signing ceremony.

US crypto regulation: the technical case is clear, the political case is being dismantled by moral-risk framing.

items.title: Samsung + SK Hynix unveiling $1T+ combined capex plans — the memory supercycle that squeezes device makers
items.idea: Samsung and SK Hynix are reportedly unveiling combined investment plans exceeding $1 trillion — driving their stocks down (Samsung -4.7%, SK Hynix -3.1%) on the announcement. This is the memory supercycle capex overhang: massive DRAM/HBM expansion that pressures device manufacturer margins because it raises the cost structure of the AI hardware stack. Combined with TSMC's reported Winbond collaboration, the semiconductor industry is executing a massive parallel memory buildout driven by AI inference demand.
items.angle: Samsung/SK Hynix -4.7%/-3.1% ON the capex announcement = market saying dilutive spending. But if AI memory demand grows as projected, this is the groundwork for the next HBM supercycle. Micron is the US expression. $MU
items.tweetA:
Samsung -4.7%. SK Hynix -3.1%. On reports they're unveiling $1T+ combined capex plans.

The market is punishing them for spending. But if AI inference demand is as large as projected, this is the foundation of the next memory supercycle.

Micron is the US expression. The Korea selloff may be the setup. $MU
items.tweetB:
The AI hardware stack needs more memory, not more compute. TSMC is reportedly building WoW DRAM stacking with Winbond. Samsung and SK Hynix are announcing $1T+ in capex.

The entire memory industry is executing simultaneously on AI demand. The question isn't whether demand is real — it's whether supply overshoots. $MU $NVDA

items.title: SpaceX (SPCX) on track for Nasdaq-100 fast-track add before July 7 open; holds 18,712 BTC
items.idea: SpaceX (SPCX) is on track to join the Nasdaq-100 via a fast-track addition before the July 7 market open, with index funds buying after the July 6 close. SpaceX holds 18,712 BTC on its balance sheet. There is also a tokenized SPCX on Solana. SpaceX's IPO was described as one of the mega-deals of H1 2026. Nasdaq-100 addition triggers automatic index-fund inflows.
items.angle: Automatic forced buying from index funds on July 6 close. This is a pure mechanical flow event, like Alphabet entering the Dow. Also: SpaceX's 18,712 BTC holding ties the defense/space economy to crypto balance sheets.
items.tweetA:
SpaceX joins the Nasdaq-100 before the July 7 open. Index funds are buyers on July 6 close.

This isn't a thesis. It's mechanics — passive money has to own it regardless of price.

Bonus: SpaceX holds 18,712 BTC. The intersection of space defense, AI infrastructure, and crypto balance sheets is getting crowded. $SPCX $BTC
items.tweetB:
Alphabet entered the Dow this week. SpaceX enters the Nasdaq-100 next week.

The composition of America's benchmark indices is changing faster than the underlying economy.

Both are companies that 10 years ago had no public market existence. Now they force-buy trillions in passive capital flows. Index inclusion is the new IPO.

items.title: Comcast plans to spin off media + tech into two separate public companies
items.idea: Comcast announced a plan to spin off its media and tech businesses into two separate public companies, on approximately a 1-year timeline. Comcast +4.4% on the announcement. The media consolidation/restructuring wave is continuing — major conglomerates are breaking apart as streaming, broadband, and content businesses have diverging capital structures and investor bases.
items.angle: Comcast spinoff is the next chapter in the death of the media conglomerate. Separately traded media vs. broadband = cleaner pure-plays. Watch for M&A speculation on the media piece. $CMCSA
items.tweetA:
Comcast is splitting into two public companies. Media goes one way. Tech/broadband goes the other.

+4.4% on the news. The market has been pricing conglomerate discount for years — now it gets the unlock.

This is the Verizon playbook in reverse: instead of buying content, unbundle it. Media consolidation wave continues. $CMCSA
items.tweetB:
Another media conglomerate breaking apart. Another day in 2026.

Comcast separating media from broadband/tech. ~1 year timeline. The logic: streaming needs a different capital structure than cable infrastructure.

The media spinoff will be the target. The broadband piece will trade as a utility. Both go up. The sum-of-parts discount closes.

items.title: US rare earth position: COVID + AI together pulled capital back to hardware/physical supply chain; space and defense built on energy and metals
items.idea: Capital Flows Research hosted Adam Rossi on the structural regime shift: the world moved from software-first (2010s) to hardware-first (2020s), driven by the combination of COVID (supply chain fragility exposed) and AI (compute requires physical infrastructure). The US strategic position in critical minerals and rare earth magnets is described as 'fragile.' The entire space and defense industrial buildout sits on top of energy and metals supply chains. A single policy headline in rare earths can ripple through the entire metals complex.
items.angle: The software→hardware regime shift is the macro thesis behind the commodity supercycle, energy buildout, and defense spending surge happening simultaneously. Rare earths are where China has the most leverage.
items.tweetA:
COVID showed us supply chains are physical. AI showed us compute requires physical infrastructure.

Together they triggered the regime shift from software-first investing to hardware-first investing.

The US rare earth position is 'fragile.' Space and defense sit on top of energy and metals. One policy headline in China = ripple through the entire metals complex.
items.tweetB:
The 2010s playbook: software eats the world, physical doesn't matter, supply chains optimize for cost.

The 2020s correction: everything we built on cheap global supply chains and Chinese rare earths is now a strategic liability.

That's not a trade. That's a decade-long capital reallocation. Defense, energy, critical minerals, reshoring — it's one thesis.

items.title: New definition of rich in 2026: $3-5M liquid + paid home = 'time rich'; time freedom > status goods
items.idea: BowTied Bull argues the 2026 definition of wealth has shifted from status goods to time sovereignty. The threshold: paid-off home + $3-5M liquid = $10K-$16.7K/month at 4% rule. Key insight: 'You're not rich if you can't wake up without an alarm on a Tuesday.' High W-2 earners ($500K+) don't feel rich because they are 'time poor, location poor, always-on, and lifestyle poor.' The 'smart new money' values $1 of online income at a premium to $1 in brick-and-mortar due to location/time freedom embedded in the structure.
items.angle: This is the cultural signal underneath the great wealth migration: HNW people optimizing for autonomy, not assets. It explains the surge in indie businesses, fund manager departures, and the entrepreneur premium. Relevant for understanding where talent flows.
items.tweetA:
New definition of rich in 2026 isn't about the car or the house.

It's: can you go to the coffee shop at 11am on a Tuesday without asking anyone's permission?

Paid home + $3-5M liquid = $10-16K/month. After that, every add-on is earned freedom. The banker earning $800K is lifestyle poor. The guy who sold his SaaS is not.
items.tweetB:
The smart new money values $1 of online income above $1 of W-2 income. Not because of taxes.

Because $1 of online income comes with location freedom, time freedom, and compounding optionality.

$1 of W-2 comes with an alarm clock, a commute, a 1-week August vacation, and a boss. The market for talent is repricing this gap in real time.

items.title: H1 2026 M&A: $2.6T announced, $4T projected for full year — Iran war impact on deals muted
items.idea: Global deal value is on track to exceed $4 trillion in 2026 — highest since 2021 — with $2.6T already announced in H1. More than half came from $5B+ transactions. The Iran war's impact on deals was 'more or less muted,' though it spurred strategic dealmaking in energy and AI-infrastructure sectors. Notable deals: SpaceX IPO, NextEra's acquisition of Dominion Energy, AI transactions. VC activity at record levels. KPMG's Dean Bell: peace with Iran would trigger 'a big bounce' in deal activity.
items.angle: Deals as a macro confidence indicator: $4T projected despite a war, hot PCE, and rate hike pricing. The animal spirits are intact at the corporate level even as retail is fearful. These don't usually diverge for long.
items.tweetA:
Global M&A on track for $4 trillion in 2026 — highest since 2021. This happened WITH an Iran war, hot PCE, and the prospect of rate hikes.

If the Iran ceasefire holds, KPMG says deals 'bounce significantly.'

The market's Fear & Greed is at Extreme Fear. Corporate deal desks are booking record activity. These don't usually diverge for long.
items.tweetB:
SpaceX IPO. NextEra acquires Dominion Energy. OpenAI $122B. Anthropic $30B. All in H1 2026.

The deal market didn't care about the Iran war. It cared about AI and energy.

$4T projected global M&A this year. At $5B+ transaction sizes, this is not retail exuberance. This is CEOs and boards betting on a decade of structural growth.

charts.desc: VC deal value by quarter — OpenAI $122B vs all-time quarterly records; IPOX-100 vs S&P 500 chart — worth opening
